source_title: SNEAKO X Professor Jiang X Dave Smith | Unity Amidst Chaos - Full Panel Discussion

interactions.summary: Sneako and Jiang set up the call immediately after Sneako reports being attacked; Sneako says reports about a knockout or tooth loss are exaggerated.
interactions.speaker_attribution: SPEAKER_02 appears to be Sneako; SPEAKER_00 appears to be Jiang.

interactions.summary: Jiang says the political temperature in the United States is high enough that his wife worries he could be attacked or refused entry if he travels there.
Sneako frames the attack as possibly organized and uses it to justify panels where ideological opponents can disagree civilly.
Jiang says he has real disagreements with Dugin but wants honest conversations with people from different political beliefs.
Sneako asks about the late Dugin conversation; Jiang says he wanted to ask how Dugin reconciles Moscow as Third Rome with earlier Christian views of Rome as Antichrist.
Dave Smith joins, checks audio, and jokes that Trump has declared victory so the discussion may already be over.
interactions.speaker_attribution: SPEAKER_01 appears to be Dave Smith.

interactions.summary: Sneako asks what Trump said; Dave explains that Maria Bartiromo reported Trump declaring the war over and treats it as Trumpian performative reality-making.
Sneako and Dave joke about Trump's social-media explanation of an image as a Red Cross doctor rather than Jesus.
Sneako establishes the shared anti-Iran-war ground; Jiang says he and Dave are anti-war and that Jiang is largely aligned with Dave's libertarian beliefs.
Dave says he knows Jiang from viral predictions and asks what, if anything, Jiang thinks he is getting wrong or missing.
Jiang says the core contention is whether the war is personality- or interest-driven, versus driven by structural imperial decline and hubris.
Dave agrees structural empire-decline dynamics are real but insists personalities, incentives, and the Israel lobby remain central causes of the Iran war.
Sneako asks Dave and then Jiang how opposing U.S. empire and the Israel lobby can be discussed without being framed as hating America.
Dave says his opposition to foreign wars comes from love of America, because the global war on terror financially and spiritually degraded the country.
Jiang says his aim is game-theoretic prediction: he uses public debate to test models of what drives human events and to improve future control.

Jiang says he can sympathize with pro-empire arguments that cannot be said publicly: empire is evil but brings status and prosperity to its beneficiaries, so Americans must ask whether they prefer Russian or Chinese hegemony.
Sneako asks Dave for predictions after crediting Jiang's Venezuela-Hormuz-oil logic as vindicated.
Dave says Trump seems desperate for a gimmick to exit the Iran war; the best case is an obvious loss, and the worst case is being kept in by Israeli pressure.
Sneako asks Jiang how Americans can prevent political violence, distrust, and social breakdown after Trump alienates his former coalition.

Jiang answers structurally: America is addicted to the petrodollar, Treasury demand, and money-printing privilege; baby boomers hold the wealth and power, would reject sacrifice, and uniquely support the Iran war and Gaza policy.
Dave agrees and expands the boomer analysis into a story of asset inflation, fiat money, and America's turn from productive capitalism to central banking and government-linked extraction.

Dave narrates America's rise from productive capacity through world-war dominance, Bretton Woods, petrodollar status, finance-government extraction, and the unfair U.S.-China consumption/production bargain.

Jiang adds the Chinese side: America offshored manufacturing to China, but China offshored elite selection and indoctrination to America, leaving China intellectually colonized even as it became richer.
Jiang tracks baby-boomer power from the Great Society through Reagan, the unipolar 1990s, Covid, Gaza campus protests, and predicts boomers will make America more authoritarian before they die.
Dave accepts Jiang's point and recalls how powerfully American culture dominated the world around the unipolar moment, using Metallica in Moscow as an example.
The call briefly drops and resumes, with Sneako joking that the CCP did not get Jiang.
Dave continues the boomer critique: American goodwill was squandered by Bush and Clinton-era empire, boomers normalized selfishness, and the same political class has stayed in charge for decades.
Dave identifies a dangerous regime dynamic: a shrinking elite generation supports the system while supermajorities suffer discontent, a pattern that often produces serious national problems.
Jiang introduces Canada as a warning case: trucker protest debanking, immigration-driven housing pressure, and Mark Carney's pro-India immigration push show how much damage boomers can do.
Jiang says Mark Carney's path in Canada shows a staged, elite-backed move toward authoritarianism, with immigration and high property prices benefiting boomers.

Dave says modern propaganda is broken, which allows conversations like this, but that breakdown may provoke authoritarian crackdowns from Trump or a future Democratic administration using liberal language.

Jiang agrees with Dave's Democrat-warning frame and predicts liberal boomers will treat conservative white men as the plague to be destroyed through digital currency, carbon tax, cancel culture, DEI, and immigration.
Sneako asks what Gen Z and Gen Alpha should do if the boomer voting class keeps voting for its own interests, including whether young people should vote left in the midterms.
Dave says voting is strategic, argues Democrats may win the midterms and Republicans deserve punishment for the war, but rejects supporting major-party insiders tied to the administration.
Dave says the task is to wake people up without letting anti-liberal reaction collapse into communism or fascism; he argues America can return to constitutional freedom instead.
Sneako asks Jiang what young people should do when political violence and job retaliation make them afraid to speak openly about the Iran war.
Jiang says America must protect the First Amendment, educate young people out of woke/DEI brainwashing, and distrust Democrats based on his Yale-shaped former liberal mindset.

Jiang says Bernie Sanders was railroaded twice by the Democratic Party, while Trump at least won the Republican primary democratically; he warns Democrats produce authority-pleasing elites without moral conviction.
Dave agrees that Democrats rigged against Bernie, then broadens the warning to DNC collusion, Russiagate, laptop suppression, and Obama's expansion of the imperial presidency.
Dave distinguishes Bush's congressional authorizations from Obama's doctrine that the war is everywhere and the president can strike anywhere without even pretending to seek authorization.
Jiang adds that during Trump's first term the mainstream press and deep state colluded, with outlets acting as stenographers for three-letter agencies.
Sneako argues for supporting outsider candidates rather than blind party loyalty because isms are captured by agencies and Trump betrayed his anti-globalist, anti-Super-PAC pitch.
Dave reconstructs the 2016 Trump appeal: he claimed independence from donor money and briefly sounded neutral on Israel-Palestine, which made him feel politically serious.

Dave says Trump angered the Israel lobby by suggesting neutrality on Israel-Palestine, then won them back at AIPAC by attacking the JCPOA, eventually taking Adelson money and becoming what he said Rubio would be.
Sneako asks for final thoughts; Jiang closes by praising the conversation as proof that people with different political allegiances can align on issues and by emphasizing the First Amendment.
Dave agrees that free speech must be preserved, noting that dissident figures can still snap back at the president and contrasting platforming culture with cancellation culture.
Dave says the point is not ideological purity over guests but preserving the ability to talk through problems and lower the temperature.
Sneako returns to his same-day attack and says Gen Z fear makes it more important to support panels and dialogue.
Dave condemns the attack as dishonorable, and Sneako adds that he was maced after being punched.
Sneako thanks Jiang and Dave, says Jiang encouraged these panels, and invites future conversations before leaving for Asia.
Jiang signs off briefly after the panel closes.

speaker_notes.note: The early speaker mapping is stable: SPEAKER_00 is Jiang and SPEAKER_02 is Sneako.
SPEAKER_01 is Dave Smith from his entrance through the Trump victory discussion.

claims.claim: Jiang treats the United States in April 2026 as politically volatile enough that public travel or border entry could carry personal risk for him.
Jiang presents cross-ideological conversation as necessary even when he strongly disagrees with another participant.
Jiang identifies an unresolved religious-political problem in Dugin's Third Rome framing: Rome can be imagined as both Christian imperial inheritance and Antichristic beast system.
Jiang thinks Orthodox-Catholic differences may be reconcilable, while Protestant-Orthodox differences are harder to reconcile.
Jiang positions himself and Dave as anti-war, with Jiang mostly aligned to Dave's libertarian beliefs.
The panel is explicitly framed as a dialogue rather than a Piers Morgan-style shouting match.
Jiang's model says declining empires start overseas wars that do not benefit them long term because structural forces and hubris drive them.
claims.temporal_scope: macro-historical pattern applied to 2026 Iran war

claims.claim: Dave argues that the Israel lobby has pushed for war with Iran for roughly 25 years and that this narrower causal story should not be ignored.
Dave treats the global war on terror as a force that bankrupted America not only financially but spiritually and culturally.
Jiang defines his predictive practice as testing theoretical models of human events rather than expressing a fixed partisan leaning.
Jiang argues that empire is evil in nature but materially benefits people inside the imperium through status and prosperity.
Jiang says the moral question for Americans is whether they would accept China or Russia controlling global institutions and setting humanity's agenda.
Dave predicts that if Trump exits the Iran war under current conditions, it will still read as an unquestionable loss that destroys his presidency.
Jiang argues that the American lifestyle is financed by petrodollar privilege, money printing, and global demand for U.S. Treasuries.
Jiang says baby boomers have the most power and wealth in America and would not accept even a hypothetical 50 percent tax to secure world peace for descendants.
Jiang claims baby boomers are the only demographic supporting the Iran war and Israel's actions in Gaza.
Dave says the United States shifted from laissez-faire productive capitalism into central banking, fiat currency, government contracting, finance, and military-linked extraction.
Dave argues that America's postwar wealth shifted from production to a system of consumption financed by Bretton Woods, petrodollar status, debt, finance, and government-linked extraction.
Dave says the American-China deal was production by China and consumption by America, with China also lending America money to consume.
Jiang says China gained manufacturing and a middle class but lost civilizational and intellectual capacity by sending its elite to U.S. universities for indoctrination.
Jiang says baby boomers enjoyed a uniquely favorable life course: Great Society social supports, Reagan-era wealth opportunities, and unipolar American privilege.
Jiang says Covid lockdowns benefited boomers with assets and health-risk concerns while damaging young people's mental health, schooling, and economic prospects.
Jiang predicts boomers will make America more authoritarian before they die so they can live peacefully in gated communities and continue extracting from the republic.
Dave says American culture had extraordinary global goodwill around the Soviet collapse, symbolized by heavy-metal enthusiasm in Moscow.
Dave says boomers are now a minority on many issues, including Israel, the Iran war, and Trump, but remain unusually powerful.
Dave's model says a shrinking elite generation backing the regime against discontented supermajorities is a historically dangerous configuration.
Jiang says Canada's Covid-era trucker protest response was far more authoritarian than the U.S. response because protesters and even donors were debanked.
Jiang argues that Canada's post-Covid immigration surge worsened jobs and housing for young people while benefiting boomers with assets.

Jiang presents Canada as America's future warning case: unelected or elite-managed leadership, opposition bribery, immigration policy, and property inflation can consolidate boomer-backed authoritarianism.
Dave says modern corporate-media propaganda control has broken enough that dissident conversations can happen outside the old controlled model.
Dave warns that future Democratic power could implement vaccine passports, central bank digital currency, carbon scores, hate-speech controls, or similar systems under liberal language.
Jiang predicts Democrats will target conservative white men as the primary threat to their worldview if they return to power.
Jiang names digital currency, carbon tax, cancel culture, DEI, and illegal immigration as tools Democrats would use against that target group.
Dave says Republicans should be punished for the Iran war and that no major Trump-administration figure should receive future support unless they break from it.
Dave argues that disillusionment with liberal-sounding empire can push young people toward communism or fascism, both of which he treats as catastrophic paths.
Dave says America's viable third path is returning to the Constitution, Bill of Rights, Declaration, and freedom rather than adopting illiberal alternatives.
Jiang says the First Amendment is what makes America great and must be protected at all costs.
Jiang says elite schools such as Yale can produce a programmed liberal mindset; he cites his own 2016 TDS as evidence.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's retrospective account of 2016 and elite education

claims.claim: Jiang says the Democratic Party did not run real democratic primaries in 2016, 2020, or 2024, and that this makes him wary of Democrats despite Republican failures.
Jiang says Harvard and Yale graduate authority-pleasers who believe ends justify means and lack moral conviction.
Dave says the 2016 DNC emails showed institutional collusion against Bernie Sanders, including debate-question leakage and smear coordination.
Dave argues that Russiagate and the Biden laptop intelligence letter were deep-state interventions that exerted control over presidential politics.
Dave says Obama made the imperial presidency naked by treating the global war on terrorism as everywhere and authorizing strikes anywhere without congressional authorization.
Jiang says mainstream outlets such as the New York Times and CNN colluded with the deep state during Trump's first term and became stenographers for intelligence agencies.
Sneako says party allegiance traps voters because political isms are subverted by federal agencies; voters should back specific outsiders and never trust blindly again.
Dave says Trump's original 2016 appeal rested partly on refusing donor money and claiming he could tell everyone whatever he wanted because he was self-funded.
Dave argues that Trump's path to the Iran conflict began when he used anti-JCPOA promises to placate the Israel lobby after briefly suggesting neutrality on Israel-Palestine.
Jiang says the First Amendment is the best thing about America and should guide whom people support politically.
Dave says losing the First Amendment would be nearly irreversible, so whoever supports free speech is the good side and whoever opposes it is the bad side.
Dave says platforming opponents is necessary because if people cannot talk through problems, they have no chance of lowering the political temperature.
Sneako treats the attack against him as political violence that demonstrates why public dialogue needs active defense.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang imagines being recognized in New York as the person who will start a war and then being attacked.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you're the ass who will start this war
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The interview opens with the stakes of prediction becoming personal exposure, not abstract commentary.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang asks how Moscow can become Third Rome when early Christians cast Rome as the beast system.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Rome was considered the Antichrist
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns the Dugin question from geopolitics into a contradiction inside Christian imperial inheritance.

signature_moments.moment: Dave invites Jiang to identify what he is missing, setting up the interview as model comparison rather than agreement theater.
signature_moments.source_phrase: what, if anything, he thinks maybe I'm getting wrong or I'm missing
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This opens the central Jiang move: shifting Dave from personality and lobby explanations toward structural macro-history.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang replaces a villain-centered explanation with an imperial-decline machine.
signature_moments.source_phrase: it's much more structural
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the central interpretive turn of the interview: Netanyahu, Trump, and lobbies matter, but Jiang asks what structure makes them legible.

signature_moments.moment: Dave says supporting the wars would be the real way to hate America.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if I hated America, I would support all of these wars
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It inverts the patriotism charge used against anti-war critics.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang says the private pro-empire argument is that empire is evil but good for those inside it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: an empire in its nature is an evil thing. But an empire brings tremendous status
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the interview a harder moral frame than simple anti-imperial denunciation: beneficiaries must face what they gain.

signature_moments.moment: Dave's best-case scenario is still Trump losing.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the best case scenario is that somehow Donald Trump can get out of this. And it's just clearly a loss
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It establishes how narrow the exit path has become in the panel's shared analysis.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang says the average American lives better than a Roman emperor.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you basically live better than Roman Emperor
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It compresses the scale of imperial privilege into a concrete image before asking who will sacrifice it.

signature_moments.moment: The baby-boomer thought experiment fails because the imagined boomer chooses another 10 or 20 years of comfort over sacrifice.
signature_moments.source_phrase: there's absolutely no way that I, as a baby boomer, would agree
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns geopolitics into intergenerational bargaining failure.

signature_moments.moment: Dave says America 'McDonald's' its way into winning two world wars through overwhelming production.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we mcdonald's our way into winning two world wars
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The line captures his contrast between mass production power and later financialized consumption.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang says China offshored its elite selection and indoctrination to America.
signature_moments.source_phrase: china offshore its elite selection and indoctrination to america
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It reverses the usual manufacturing-offshoring story and claims China paid with its elite mind.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang calls boomers parasites on the American Republic who will harden the state before they die.
signature_moments.source_phrase: continue to be parasites on the American Republic
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the sharpest generational accusation in the source and becomes a prediction about political form.

signature_moments.moment: Dave's image of Metallica in Moscow carries the vanished appeal of American culture.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Loving American heavy metal
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the decline story a cultural memory rather than only balance sheets and wars.

signature_moments.moment: Dave describes a small elite generation defending the regime against discontented supermajorities.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a smaller and smaller group of elites
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It translates the boomer critique into a general instability model.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang uses Canadian debanking to show that authoritarianism can arrive through banking access before overt police rule.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if you support them in any way... you are debanked
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the authoritarian prediction a concrete institutional mechanism.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang says Canada could be America's future, not its contrast.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Canada could be America's savior. It's Canada's future.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The line makes Canada a predictive laboratory for American authoritarian drift.

signature_moments.moment: Dave says even coercion in America arrives wrapped in freedom language.
signature_moments.source_phrase: They call your income tax voluntary compliance
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It captures the panel's shared suspicion that U.S. authoritarianism hides inside liberal vocabulary.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang casts liberal-boomer politics as a plague hunt against conservative white men.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the disease, the plague in America are conservative white men
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is Jiang's starkest formulation of the domestic enemy-image he expects Democrats to mobilize.

signature_moments.moment: Dave says he does not want the smell of supporting Trump on him again.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I just don't want that filthy smell on me again
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It marks the emotional break between anti-war dissidents and the Trump coalition.

signature_moments.moment: Dave says the American model can be the third way: enforce the Constitution again.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the constitution is still the law of the land
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the anti-war coalition a positive program rather than only refusal.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang names the First Amendment as the thing America has that Canada and China do not.
signature_moments.source_phrase: no other nation in the world has this
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It clarifies that Jiang's critique of America is not anti-American; he isolates free speech as its civilizational treasure.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang calls elite-school graduates ass-kissers trained to suck up to authority.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they graduate ass kissers
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It ties his distrust of Democrats to an elite-formation theory, not just party preference.

signature_moments.moment: Dave defines the Obama doctrine as war everywhere, anytime.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the war is everywhere
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It names the precedential expansion of executive war power that the panel fears returning to Democrats.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang says media-deep-state collusion is a direct threat to American democracy.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a direct threat to american democracy
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows Jiang defending American democratic structure while criticizing its institutions.

signature_moments.moment: Dave remembers Trump pausing and inventing an Israel-Palestine neutrality answer in real time.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we should be neutral with israel and palestine
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It sets up the later reversal where Trump becomes what he accused Rubio of being.

signature_moments.moment: Dave says Trump became everything he claimed Rubio would be.
signature_moments.source_phrase: be everything he claimed marco rubio was going to be
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It crystallizes the betrayal arc of Trump from donor-independent outsider to lobby-bound war president.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang closes on the First Amendment as America's irreplaceable political achievement.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we don't have it in canada or china or anywhere else in the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang's public critique a pro-American anchor.

signature_moments.moment: Dave says people must be allowed to talk problems through or they have no chance.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we got to be allowed to talk this through
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns the panel's format into its substantive political answer.

signature_moments.moment: Sneako looks at blood on his hands while arguing for more dialogue.
signature_moments.source_phrase: there's blood on my fingers
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The source ends by making political speech physically costly and therefore more urgent.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A measure of public volatility high enough to affect travel, speech, and personal safety.
Moscow's claimed inheritance of Rome's Christian imperial role, which Jiang tests against Protestant and early Christian suspicion of Rome.
A cross-ideological alignment against the Iran war, including Jiang and Dave despite different political backgrounds.
Jiang's claim that wars of imperial decline arise from macro-historical structure and hubris, not only leaders or lobbies.
The interior of empire whose inhabitants receive status and prosperity from an otherwise evil imperial structure.
Jiang's phrase for a post-hegemonic America retreating back into its own continental space.
Jiang's shorthand for U.S. dependence on dollar-centered oil and Treasury demand to finance a privileged lifestyle.
A thought experiment asking whether the wealthiest generation would accept large taxes for peace and future generations; Jiang's answer is no.
Jiang's term for Chinese elites absorbing neoliberal, pro-American assumptions through U.S. universities and then governing China through them.
Dave's account of the China-U.S. relationship: China makes goods, America consumes them and borrows to keep consuming.
Jiang's prediction that aging boomers will preserve comfort by making America more authoritarian before leaving power.
The period after the Soviet collapse when U.S. power and culture appeared globally dominant.
Removal from banking access as punishment for protest or support for protest; Jiang treats it as authoritarian control.
A political structure where a shrinking elite group supports the regime against broad popular discontent.
Dave's model that American coercion, especially for boomers, must be justified through liberal terms such as freedom, safety, hate speech, or climate.
Sneako's term for older voters who still dominate elections and vote their generational interests.
Dave's account of youth rejecting false liberalism by embracing communism or fascism.
Jiang's claim that free-speech protection is America's unique and best political achievement.
Dave's phrase for presidential war authority operating globally without meaningful congressional constraint.
Jiang's description of elite university graduates who serve power and justify means by ends.
Shorthand for U.S. intelligence/security agencies that Jiang and Dave say shaped media and presidential politics.
Dave's causal story that Trump's anti-Iran-deal promise won lobby approval and became an early step toward the later Iran conflict.
Dave's split between people who object to controversial guests and people who see broad conversation as necessary.

chronology_notes.note: By 2026-04-15 Jiang says he is hesitant to do a U.S. podcast circuit because the domestic temperature keeps rising.
Dave references a Trump interview reported for airing at 6 a.m. after 2026-04-15, where Trump was said to declare the war over.
Dave says he had seen Jiang's viral war predictions and describes several of them as having come true by 2026-04-15.
Sneako cites U.S. debt at 39 trillion dollars and Trump attacking the pope as live 2026 signs of imperial instability.
Sneako explicitly links earlier Venezuela regime-change discussion to the Iran/Hormuz oil thesis and says the panel believes it has been vindicated.
Sneako dates the discussion amid alienation of Tucker Carlson, Candace Owens, Megyn Kelly, Alex Jones, and Catholics from Trump over the war and rhetoric toward the pope.
Jiang says the intellectual effects of China's U.S.-educated elite could hinder Chinese development for the next few hundred years.
Jiang's generational timeline runs Great Society to Reagan to the 1990s unipolar moment to Trump 2016, Covid, October 7, and campus protest crackdowns.
Jiang's Canada warning links Covid lockdown politics, the trucker protest, post-Covid immigration, and Mark Carney's India outreach into one authoritarian-boomer sequence.
Dave cites Biden's attempted vaccine mandate for large employers, CBDC discussion, and vaccine passport ideas as recent precedents for future controls.
Dave expects Democrats to win the 2026 midterms but distinguishes that from giving Democrats the White House and both chambers.
Sneako situates youth fear after the assassination of Charlie Kirk and current job-related fear over stating anti-war positions.
Jiang recounts moving from 2016 anti-Trump liberal reflex through close attention to the 2020 Democratic primary and skepticism toward Democratic elite selection.
Dave frames 2016 Trump as a coming-of-age political moment for younger dissidents because Trump attacked donor capture and briefly seemed independent on Israel-Palestine.
Dave traces a sequence from 2016 neutrality language, to AIPAC and JCPOA opposition, to Adelson money, to the 2026 Iran war.
The final exchange repeatedly ties the panel to an attack on Sneako that occurred about three hours before the conversation.

uncertainty_notes.note: Segment 0038 contains repeated ASR text around 'destroying Iran's nuclear' and should not be overread beyond Dave's stated war-goal discussion.
Segment 0046 contains ASR noise and repeated filler; use only the clear beginning where Dave agrees with Jiang's baby-boomer point.
Dave expresses uncertainty about claims regarding China's social credit system and Uyghur numbers; preserve that as Dave's uncertainty rather than a settled Jiang claim.
